coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots assisted BiFeO3@BiOBr S-scheme heterojunction enhanced peroxymonosulfate activation for the photocatalytic degradation of imidacloprid under visible light: Performance, mechanism and biotoxicity
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Sai
Lv, Ting
Chen, Miaomiao
Li, Chang
Wang, Zichen
Yang, Xinyao
Xia, Tianjiao
coredata.dc:description: A novel S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/BiFeO3/BiOBr (CBB) was synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method, which was highly effective in activating peroxymonosulfate (PMS) to photodegrade imidacloprid (IMD) (one of the typical neonicotinoid insecticides (NEOs)) under visible light irradiation. Based on the physicochemical and photoelectrochemical analysis, the super photocatalytic performance of the CBB photocatalyst was contributed to the enhanced separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+), the activation of PMS by reactive species, and the wider light absorption range induced by CQDs. Moreover, the intermediate products and possible photodegradation pathways of IMD were confirmed through high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) detection and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Although the photodegradation of IMD in the CBB/PMS/Vis system can be affected by the water quality parameters (i.e., acid group anions, pH, and the presence of humic acid (HA)), the synthesized CBB photocatalyst showed excellent photocatalytic performance in multiple natural water samples. This study provides a new idea to construct an effective and efficient heterojunction photocatalyst, which may have great advantages in photocatalytic degradation of NEOs and possibly other emerging contaminants in the aquatic environment.

(a) XRD patterns; (b) FT-IR spectra; (c) XPS wide spectra; XPS fine spectra of (c) C 1 s, (d) Fe 2p and (e) Br 3d of the as-synthesized materials.
(a) UV–vis DRS and (b) Eg of the photocatalysts; VB-XPS curves of (c) BFO and (d) BOB; Mott-schottky curves of (e) BFO and (f) BOB.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation curves and (b) corresponding kinetic linear fitting curves of IMD as catalyzed by different photocatalysts under visible light after 180 min of irradiation; effects of (c) PMS concentration and (d) catalysts dosage on IMD photodegradation (pH = 7.1, temperature = 25 °C).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots assisted BiFeO3@BiOBr S-scheme heterojunction enhanced peroxymonosulfate activation for the photocatalytic degradation of imidacloprid under visible light: Performance, mechanism and biotoxicity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/BiFeO3/BiOBr (CBB) was synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method, which was highly effective in activating peroxymonosulfate (PMS) to photodegrade imidacloprid (IMD) (one of the typical neonicotinoid insecticides (NEOs)) under visible light irradiation. Based on the physicochemical and photoelectrochemical analysis, the super photocatalytic performance of the CBB photocatalyst was contributed to the enhanced separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+), the activation of PMS by reactive species, and the wider light absorption range induced by CQDs. Moreover, the intermediate products and possible photodegradation pathways of IMD were confirmed through high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) detection and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Although the photodegradation of IMD in the CBB/PMS/Vis system can be affected by the water quality parameters (i.e., acid group anions, pH, and the presence of humic acid (HA)), the synthesized CBB photocatalyst showed excellent photocatalytic performance in multiple natural water samples. This study provides a new idea to construct an effective and efficient heterojunction photocatalyst, which may have great advantages in photocatalytic degradation of NEOs and possibly other emerging contaminants in the aquatic environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Neonicotinoid insecticides (NEOs) are neurotoxic pesticides that strongly bind to nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) of the nervous system (Zhang et al., 2022). Because of their broad-spectrum, strong absorbency and high insecticidal activity, NEOs have largely replaced traditional organochlorine and organophosphorus pesticides and account for nearly a quarter of the current market share of insecticides (Mahai et al., 2021). Residual NEOs that have not been completely absorbed and metabolized are easy to migrate and spread along the water-soil-biosphere cycle, thus cause potential environmental and human health risks. Imidacloprid (IMD), as one of the most widely used NEOs, has been frequently detected in water and soil systems due to its high water solubility and environmental persistence (Kitsiou et al., 2009; Tan et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2023). A number of studies have confirmed the ecological threats of IMD to aquatic organisms and birds (Gibbons et al., 2015; Jensen, 2019; Naumann et al., 2021), which may affect the ecological balance of nature. Thus, the toxicity and resistance problems of NEOs have aroused increasing public attentions.

Photocatalytic technology is one of the most promising advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), which has been worldwide applied for the elimination of recalcitrant organic molecules from the aqueous solutions. A number of visible-light-driven materials that exhibit perfect optical, electronic, magnetic and structural properties, such as CdS (Chen et al., 2013; Prakash et al., 2023), Ag3PO4 (Shi et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2020) and g-C3N4 (Qu et al., 2018; Xiao et al., 2023) have been selected as effective photocatalysts. However, these photocatalysts often possess poor photochemical stability and relatively narrow light response range, which largely limits their photocatalytic activity.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles with diameters of 2–10 nm, which comprise of amorphous nanocrystalline cores and graphitic sheets (sp2 carbon) fused by diamond-like sp3 hybridized carbon insertions (Wang et al., 2019). It has been reported that CQDs possess excellent electrical conductivity, electronic reservoir capability, photostability and up-conversion of photoluminescence (PL) (Qin and Zeng, 2017; Wang et al., 2018). Furthermore, CQDs are easy to combine with other photocatalysts via π-π or H-bonds interactions owing to their π-conjugated structure and surface O-functional groups, which assembles the advantages of various materials to make the obtained composite catalysts excellent photocatalytic performance (Qin and Zeng, 2017).

Bi-based materials with unique stratified framework, adjustable electronic band structure, favorable stability and hypotoxicity have attracted extensive concerns in the photocatalysis field (Li et al., 2019a; Peng et al., 2023; Xing et al., 2021). However, the relative low light absorption efficiency and substantial recombination of the photo-generated carriers restrict the photocatalytic activity of a single bismuth-based photocatalyst (Khan et al., 2019). In this context, heterojunctions were created via coupling bismuth-based photocatalysts with other semiconductors (e.g., TiO2, ZnIn2S4, Ag3PO4, AgI, CuS, LaFeO3 and BaTiO3) to further enhance the photoactivity of the pristine catalysts (Bao and Chen, 2018; Bhoi and Mishra, 2018; Li et al., 2019b; Wang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2023a).

This work presents a simple hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of a ternary CQDs/BiFeO3/BiOBr heterojunction, which exhibits superior photocatalytic performance toward IMD degradation through peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation under visible light irradiation. Investigations are conducted on the structure, morphologies, optical and electrochemical characteristics of the photocatalyst. Free radicals trapping experiments, high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) detection and density functional theory (DFT) calculations are used to uncover the possible photodegradation mechanism and pathways. The toxicity of the intermediate products was evaluated. The photocatalytic performance of the catalyst as affected by different water quality parameters (i.e., acid group anions, pH and the presence of humic acid (HA)) and in natural water samples was explored.
The reagents and chemicals used in this study were listed in Supplementary Information (SI) (Text S1).

CQDs were prepared via a hydrothermal carbonization process. BiFeO3 and BiOBr (referred to as BFO and BOB hereafter) were synthesized through a polyacrylamide gel and a hydrothermal method, respectively. The detail procedure was provided in Text S2. CQDs/BiFeO3/BiOBr (referred to as CBB hereafter) photocatalyst was prepared using a hydrothermal method. Typically, 3 mM of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 2 mM of KBr were orderly added into 30 mL acetic acid (CH3COOH) (5 %), which was kept sonicating and stirring for 30 min. Then, 2 mM of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and 20 mg of CQDs were added into the solution. The mixture was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 3 h. The obtained CBB photocatalyst was oven-dried at 70 °C for 12 h and stored for future use.

Morphology features of the as-prepared materials were visualized through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) (Nano SEM-450, FEI, USA), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (TECNAI G2 SPIRIT BIO, FEI, USA). Crystallinity of the materials was determined by X-ray Diffraction (XRD) equipped with a diffractometer using monochromatized Cu-Kα radiation (D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany). Elemental composition and O-functionalities distribution of the samples were measured via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra (Vetex 70, Bruker, Germany) and X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo Scientific, USA). In-situ Raman spectra (HR Evolution, HORIBA, Japan) was conducted to investigate the decomposition of PMS. Optical properties of the catalysts were detected through UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS) (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan). The Mott-Schottky plots, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), photocurrent response, cycle voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were obtained by an electrochemical workstation equipped with a standard three-electrode model (CHI 660E, Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China). Detailed method for the electrochemical tests was exhibited in Text S3, and calculations of band gap energy (Eg), conduction band potential (ECB) and valence band potential (EVB) were presented in Text S4. PL spectroscopy was recorded using Transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh, UK).

Photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared catalysts was evaluated by the degradation of IMD in the PMS systems under visible light irradiation. A 500 W Xe lamp with a 400 nm cut-off filter was applied to simulate visible light. 50 mL solution containing 10 mg/L of IMD was placed in quartz tubes that equipped on a photochemical reactor (Xujiang Electromechanical Plant, China). Selected weights of photocatalysts and PMS were added into the tubes and kept stirring in dark for 60 min before the formal photocatalytic experiments. 1 mL aliquots were withdrawn at certain intervals, and then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min and filtered through 0.22 μm polyether sulfone filters (Luer, USA). The concentrations and degradation intermediates of IMD were determined by ultrahigh performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC) (Ulitimate-3000, Thermo Scientific, USA) and HPLC-MS (UPLC-QTOF/MS, Agilent, USA), respectively. Detailed methods were listed in SI (Texts S5 and S6). The degradation of IMD was fitted by the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation:
(1)
ln
C /
C 0
= − kt
where C (mg/L) and C 0 (mg/L) are the real-time and initial concentrations of IMD, respectively, and k (min−1) is the reaction rate constant. The procedures of the cycling experiment and leaching experiment were showed in Text S7.

In order to explore the efficient reactive species during the photocatalytic process, quenchers of 100 mM of isopropyl alcohol (IPA), 100 mM of tert-butanol (TBA), 20 mM of p-benzoquinone (BQ), 10 mM of ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid-disodium (EDTA-2Na), 5 mM of furfuryl alcohol (FFA) were added into the photocatalytic systems to scavenge hydroxyl radical (·OH) and sulfate radical (SO4 ·-), ·OH, superoxide radical (O2 ·-), hole (h+) and singlet oxygen (1O2), respectively. The experiments were carried out in triplicate. The photodegradation efficiency of IMD was determined through HPLC detection. The methods for calculating the contributions of the reactive species were presented in Text S8. EPR (EMXplus-6/1, Bruker, Germany) was used for reactive oxygen species (ROS) detection. The concentration of 1O2 was determined using molecule probe. Details were presented in Text S9.

The geometric structure optimization and frequency calculations of IMD were predicted via B3LYP functional at the 6-31G(d) basis set by employing Gaussian 09 software package (Peng et al., 2021). The Fukui index based on the Hirsheld charges and the electrostatic potential (ESP) of IMD were calculated using software Multiwfn version 3.8 (Lu and Chen, 2012). The work functions of the catalysts were investigated by Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) using the Perdew-Bruke-Ernzerhof (PBE) function of the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) (Qi et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023b; Wang et al., 2019). Detailed information was presented in SI (Text S10).

The morphology and element distribution of the as-synthesized materials were examined using SEM-EDS as presented in Fig. 1 . Briefly, pure CQDs was consisted of approximate cubes (Fig. 1a), BFO showed a flower-like nanostructures with a diameter of 2–3 μm (Fig. 1b), and BOB presented thin sheet shape ranging from 0.1 to 1 μm (Fig. 1c). The SEM image of CBB clearly exhibited that the microspheres of CQDs cubes, BFO and BOB sheets were closely interconnected (Fig. 1d). Figs. 1e-1j showed the EDS spectrum and corresponding elemental mapping of the CBB hybrid, and the detailed element ratios were listed in Table S1. The distinct peaks in the EDS spectrum demonstrated the co-presence of O, Fe, Br and Bi elements in CBB, indicating the combination of BFO and BOB in the hybrid.

TEM and HR-TEM were applied to further visualize the interfaces of the materials, which showed that CQDs, BFO and BOB were closely adhered in the CBB hybrid (Fig. S1a-S1d). The polycrystalline structure of CBB was inferred from the HR-TEM image (Fig. S1e), in that the characteristic lattice spacing of 0.317 nm is indexed to the (002) facet of CQDs, the lattice spacings of 0.278 and 0.282 nm correspond to the (−110) and (110) facets of BFO, and the lattice spacings of 0.227 and 0.263 nm are assigned to the (112) and (111) facets of BOB, respectively. Together with the selected area of electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. S1f), these results further confirmed the co-existence of CQDs, BFO, and BOB in the CBB hybrid.

The XRD spectra of the materials were displayed in Fig. 2a. The distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ of 22.42°, 31.75°, 39.50°, 45.77°, 51.32°, 56.35°, 57.21°, 70.61° and 71.37° correspond to the (010), (1 1 0), (−1 1 0), (−111), (020), (120), (121), (−1−12) and (221) planes, respectively, which match well with those of rhombohedral phase BFO (JCPDS. No. 74–2016) (Chi et al., 2015). The diffraction peaks at 2θ of 10.91°, 25.20°, 32.24°, 46.24°, 50.66°, 53.38°, 57.16°, 67.47° and 71.00° are attributed to the (001), (101), (1 1 0), (200), (1 0 4), (211), (212), (220) and (2 1 4) planes of tetragonal phase BOB (JCPDS. No. 78–0348) (Henríquez et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2023). Similar characteristic XRD peaks could also be observed in the CBB hybrid.

The chemical bonds and functional groups of CQDs, BFO, BOB and CBB were detected via FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2b, broad absorption peaks corresponding to the typical stretch vibration of OH were observed at 3350–3560 cm−1, and the peaks at 1726 cm−1 and 1182 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of CO and the bending vibration of CO (Mahmoud et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2023b). Notably, the BiO vibration at 512 cm−1 and the FeO vibration at 555 cm−1 in CBB shifted toward lower wavelength compared with BOB and BFO, indicating obvious interactions among BOB and BFO and the successful formation of the heterojunction in CBB.

XPS was carried out to analyze the valence state and chemical composition of the materials. The full spectra confirmed that the C, Fe, Br, Bi and O elements containing in CQDs, BFO and BOB were also discovered in CBB (Fig. 2c). As shown in the C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2d), the three peaks at approximate 284.79, 286.39 and 288.99 eV were associated with CO, CO and C-C/C=C groups, respectively, which were consistent with the structures of CQDs (Brachi, 2020; Mahmoud et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2021). It can be observed from Fig. 2e that the peaks at 709.79 and 711.47 eV correspond to Fe 2p3/2, and the peaks at 718.58 and 724.10 eV were assigned to Fe 2p1/2 (Bhoi and Mishra, 2018; Peng et al., 2023; Wong et al., 2022). Along with the satellite peaks at 714.40 and 730.4 eV, the results demonstrated the existence of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in the spinel structure of BFO (Yao et al., 2022). After the formation of CBB, the Fe3+ peaks shifted to lower energies, signifying the occurrence of electrons (e−) transfer during the synthesis of the heterojunction (Peng et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2022). Similarly, the Br 3d peaks of BOB at 68.53 and 68.28 eV (corresponding to Br 3d3/2 and Br 3d5/2, respectively (Dou et al., 2022a; Peng et al., 2023)) moved to higher energies (69.56 and 68.71 eV) as the synthesis of CBB (Fig. 2f), indicating the transfer of e− from BOB to BFO or CQDs. In Fig. S2a, the Bi 4f spectra exhibited characteristic peaks of Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 at 164.45 and 158.69 eV for BOB, and at 165.51 and 159.41 eV for BFO, verifying the existence of Bi3+ in both of BOB and BFO (Long et al., 2021; Xing et al., 2021). The Bi 4f peaks of CBB located between those of BOB and BFO, further demonstrating the formation of the CBB heterojunction (Liu and Tang, 2020). Judging from the O 1 s spectra (Fig. S2b), the peaks located at 529.23, 531.36 and 532.99 eV for BFO were attributed to lattice oxygen, hydroxyl groups and surface adsorbed oxygen, respectively, while the peaks at 530.14 and 531.30 eV for BOB can be assigned to lattice oxygen and OH bonds, respectively (Ye et al., 2013). Noticeably, compared with the components, the peak areas of CBB increased visibly, confirming the presence of more vacancies for oxygen adsorption during the formation of CBB (Wu et al., 2017). Summaries can be drawn from the XPS results that there were strong interactions among CQDs, BFO and BOB, which might benefit the transfer of photogenerated substances in the CBB heterojunction and thus facilitate its photocatalytic activity.

The optical properties of the photocatalysts were evaluated by UV–vis DRS. As shown in Fig. 3a, CQDs exerted strong adsorption in both of UV and visible light ranges. BFO and BOB exhibited evident and steep absorption edges at 550 and 435 nm, respectively. As a typical perovskite oxide, the intense visible light absorption of BFO was primarily caused by the electronic transition from VB of O2p to CB of Fe3d (Peng et al., 2023; Saravanakumar and Park, 2021). Obviously, the visible light absorption capability of CBB was significantly broadened, which demonstrated that the optical properties of the precursor materials were improved by hybridized with the semiconductors.

The Eg values of BFO, BOB and CBB were calculated as 2.21, 2.86 and 1.86 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b), revealing that the synthesis of the heterojunction narrowed the band gap energy of the catalysts and enhanced the efficiency of light utilization. VB XPS curves were used to further explore the valence band potentials of BFO and BOB. As illustrated (Fig. 3c and d), the valence bands were 0.96 eV and 2.27 eV, respectively. It can be confirmed from the positive curve slope of the Mott-Schottky plots that both of BFO and BOB are n-type semiconductors (Fig. 3e and f). The ECB and EVB values of BFO were calculated as −1.25 and 0.96 eV (vs. NHE) (Saravanakumar and Park, 2021), while those of BOB were −0.59 and 2.27 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. Based on the above analyses, the band structures of the materials were illustrated in Fig. S3.

The photocatalytic performances of the as-synthesized materials were evaluated via IMD degradation under visible light irradiation. Pre-experiments have confirmed that the photocatalysts and IMD attained a balance in terms of absorption and desorption within 60 min, in dark, and the adsorption amount of IMD by the photocatalysts only accounted for 5.4 %, 8.7 %, and 4.1 % for BOB, BFO and CBB, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4a, IMD was difficult to be degraded only with PMS under visible light. However, the photocatalysts could hasten the activation of PMS and facilitate the photodegradation of IMD. The degradation rate of IMD in the presence of BFO and BOB sharply increased to 71.7 % and 64.9 %, respectively. Surprisingly, after these two semiconductors combining with CQDs to form CBB heterojunction, the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst largely upgraded, in that the concentration of IMD nearly reduced by 95.7 % as catalyzed by CBB within 180 min. Meanwhile, the pseudo-first-order model fitting results in Fig. 4b and Table S2 indicated that CBB exhibited the optimal photocatalytic performance among the three catalysts. The kinetic rate constant (k) of IMD degradation with CBB was calculated as 0.0202 min−1, which was 2.3- and 3.3- times larger than those of BFO (0.0089 min−1) and BOB (0.0062 min−1), respectively. Noted that the obtained CBB heterojunction in this study exerted more superior photocatalytic performance compared with other photocatalysts reported in previous studies (Table S3).

The effects of PMS concentration and catalyst dosage on the photodegradation of IMD were explored to further evaluate the photocatalytic activity of CBB. As presented in Fig. 4c and d, the removal rate of IMD increased with the concentration of PMS to a maximum of 92.5 % at 500 ppm of PMS, and then decreased subsequently. The reason might be that the available active sites on CBB failed to activate surplus PMS species, thus the rest of SO4 ·- reacted with the additional HSO5 − and self-quenching became dominant (Dou et al., 2022b; Xin et al., 2023). Similarly, the optimal degradation effect of IMD was found as catalyzed by 1000 ppm of CBB. As the dosage of CBB further increased, the degradation of IMD was inhibited, which was possibly because that excessive CBB could induce light-screening effect and restrain the absorption of visible light. The corresponding kinetic curves and parameters were presented detailedly in Fig. S4, Tables S4 and S5.

The recyclability and stability of CBB were examined to guarantee the practical application of the photocatalyst. As a result, the removal efficiency of IMD did not decrease significantly after four subsequent cycles (Fig. S5). Meanwhile, the XRD patterns and FT-IR spectra of CBB exhibited indistinctive changes before and after the four-cycle experiment (Figs. S6 and S7). Besides, the result of leaching experiment showed that there was scarcely leaching content of Fe and Bi during the photocatalytic reaction, with the concentrations of Fe and Bi only increased 3.8 % and 2.9 %, respectively, after the photocatalytic process (Fig. S8). These results revealed that the synthesized CBB catalyst has a good prospect in practical application.

Radical trapping experiments were conducted to uncover the predominant reactive species during the photocatalytic process. Judging from the degradation kinetics (Fig. 5a, b and Table S6) and relative contributions of the reactive species (Table S7), it can be observed that the addition of IPA, EDTA-2Na and FFA, which serve as quenchers of SO4 ·- and ·OH, h+, and 1O2, respectively, dramatically restrained the photodegradation of IMD. The removal efficiency of IMD also decreased obviously after the addition of BQ (trapping agent of·O2 −). However, the impact of TBA (trapping agent of ·OH) on the photodegradation of IMD was insignificant. These results indicated that CBB with activated PMS could produce reactive species such as h+, SO4 ·-, 1O2 and O2 ·-, and thus significantly improved the photocatalytic degradation of IMD. The generation of the above reactive species during the photocatalytic degradation can also be verified by the EPR results (Figs. 5c-5e), which showed that the response signal of 1O2 was the highest. This demonstrated the greatest contribution of 1O2 in the CBB/PMS/Vis system, which was in accordance with the quantification of 1O2 within 30 min during the photocatalytic process (Fig. 5f).

Conventional type-II photocatalytic mechanism cannot well explain the superior photocatalytic performance of CBB, in that the CB potential of BOB and the VB potential of BFO fail to meet the potentials of SO4 ·- produced by PMS and ·OH produced by H2O (Duan et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2021). In this case, S-scheme heterojunction mechanism seems more plausible and convincing. FT-IR and XPS results have verified the occurrence of e− transfer between BFO and BOB (Figs. 2b-2f). This would generate an inner electric field in the heterojunction, in which the band edge of BOB bended downward due to the loss of e−, while the band edge of BFO bended upward due to the accumulation of e−. Under irradiation, the e− on the VB of BOB and BFO were photo-excited and transited to the CB so that the photogenerated h+ were left on the VB. The photogenerated e− on the CB of BOB and the h+ on the VB of BFO were inclined to recombine because of the inner electric field in the heterojunction. The e− and h+ with strong redox capacity could play important roles in the photocatalytic degradation of IMD. Firstly, PMS was activated by e− on the CB of BFO to generate HSO5 − and further altered to SO4 ·-, which benefited the photocatalytic degradation of IMD. The consumption of PMS could be confirmed by the in-situ Raman spectra (Fig. S9), in which the peaks at 881 cm−1 are associated with the adsorbed PMS*(HSO5 −), and the peaks at 1060 and 982 cm−1 represent HSO4 − and SO4 2−, respectively (Lee et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2022). Secondly, the e− on the CB of BFO were captured by O2 to produce O2 ·-, which could oxidize IMD into small molecules, and the h+ on the VB of BOB with oxidation potential would directly take part in the photocatalytic oxidation of IMD. Thirdly, the photogenerated h+ could interact with H2O/OH− to generate ·OH, and further reacted with HSO5 − or H2O2 to generate SO4 ·- or O2 ·-/1O2, respectively (Xu et al., 2021), which participated in the oxydative degradation of IMD. Besides, CQDs could enhance the range of light absorption, owing to their up-conversion effect caused by two-photon absorption. When excited by visible or near-infrared light, the e− in the π orbital of CQDs could jump to the high energy π⁎ orbital, and excited e− would return to the low energy σ orbital due to radiation relaxation (Qi et al., 2022), thus the emission of short wavelength light was largely facilitated and the light absorption and photocatalytic activity of CBB were enhanced. Based on the above analysis, a rational S-scheme photocatalytic mechanism for CBB heterojunction was illustrated in Fig. 6 .

To further understand the superior photocatalytic performance of the S-scheme heterojunction, PL, EIS, photocurrent response, CV and LSV curves measurements were carried out (Fig. S10). In detail, an attenuated PL signal was observed for CBB in comparison with BFO and BOB (Fig. S10a), indicating restrained recombination of photogenerated substances of the heterojunction. In the EIS Nyquist plots (Fig. S10b), the smaller arc radius of CBB than those of the components reflected its better charge separation and transfer efficiency. In the transient photocurrent measurement (Fig. S10c), CBB exhibited the strongest response among the three catalysts, implying that the hybridization of CQDs with BFO and BOB enhanced the separation and transfer of the photogenerated carriers and thus improved the photocurrent response of the material. The CV and LSV curves (Figs. S10d and S10e) also showed the strongest photocurrent density of CBB, which demonstrated the excellent e− transmission capability and photoelectrochemical performance of the heterojunction. Based on the DFT calculations, the work functions of BOB (2 0 0) and BFO (0 1 0) were calculated as 5.71 and 5.50 eV, respectively (Figs. S11a and S11b), indicating that BOB had a lower Fermi energy (E f) before contact with BFO (Fig. S11c). After contact in dark, the e− of BFO spontaneously transferred to BOB crossing the interface until the Ef achieved balance (Fig. S11d). The interface regions near BFO accumulated positive charges due to the loss of e−, resulting in upward bending of the band edges. Instead, regions around BOB had negative charges with e− accumulation, leading to downward bending of the band edge (Zhou et al., 2022). Therefore, an internal electric field was built. Once exposed to irradiation, the e− could be easily photoexcited by CQDs (Feng et al., 2020). Subsequently, the photogenerated e− shifted oppositely from BOB to BFO through CQDs, and thus the S-scheme transfer channel was generated (Fig. S11e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation pathways of IMD and toxicity assessment of the products
ce:section.ce:para:
DFT calculations were conducted to obtain the Fukui index on nucleophilic (f
+ ), electrophilic attack (f
− ) and radical attack (f 0) of IMD molecule according to Hirshfeld charge distribution, which helps to elucidate the possible photodegradation pathway of IMD in the CBB/PMS/Vis system. The higher the f + and f − values are, the more vulnerable the atomic sites are attacked by nucleophilic and electrophilic species (Du et al., 2022; Gao et al., 2021). However, some atomic sites have the contradiction of nucleophilicity and electrophilicity at the same time, f 0 value was consequently calculated to comprehensively consider the radicals attack reaction.

Judging from the DFT results (Figs. 7a-7d and Table S8) and the main intermediate products (Table S9), the possible photodegradation routes of IMD as catalyzed by CBB were proposed (Fig. 7e). In pathway I, C8 and C11 with relatively high f + values (0.0511 and 0.0893) were liable to be attacked by nucleophilic O2 ·-, resulting the formation of intermediates P1 (m/z = 274.3) and P2 (m/z = 287.2) (Tan et al., 2022). Then nitro group was removed under the attack of O2 ·- to obtain P3 (m/z = 212). CC bond in the imidazole ring collapsed to form P4 (m/z = 185.1) and was further converted to P5 (m/z = 114.1). Pathway II mainly contained dechlorination and hydroxylation. O17 of IMD molecule with relatively high f − value (0.1070) was easily attacked by electrophilic species h+, 1O2 and SO4 ·-, resulting in the interruption of NO bond and then the generation of intermediate P6 (m/z = 239.2). Cl3 with the highest f + (0.1281) had a great possibility to be attacked by O2 ·-, which led to the cleavage of CCl bond joining with the pyridine ring and the occurrence of intermediate P7 (m/z = 205.1). N15 with the highest f − (0.1783) and f 0 (0.1251) was easily attacked by 1O2 and SO4 ·-, after which P8 (m/z = 191.9) was produced through NN bond cleavage and hydroxylation and further converted to P9 (m/z = 178) and P10 (m/z = 174.9) under the attack of h+, 1O2, SO4 ·- and O2 ·-. Then reactive species attacked the CC bond to degrade the intermediates into smaller molecules P11 (m/z = 78.9), P12 (m/z = 86.8) and P13 (m/z = 86). In pathway III, -N2O group in P6 was attacked by O2 ·- to form P14 (m/z = 212.8) as the nitro group was eliminated. Similar reaction occurred in the further degradation of the IMD urea, which led to the generation of P15 (m/z = 225.1). Then the CC bond between pyridine and pyrazole was broken, and the intermediates P5 (m/z = 114.1) and P16 (m/z = 129.1) appeared.

The highest occupied molecular orbits (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbits (LUMO) electronic transition was proposed to identify the activation of PMS by photogenerated e−, and to confirm the locations where molecules might gain or lose e− during the photocatalytic reaction (Wang et al., 2017). As shown in Figs. S12a and S12b, the HOMO of IMD was primarily localized on Cl3, the pyridine ring and the imidazole ring, which were easily attacked by electrophilic species, while the LUMO was located on the nitro group and the sites of N9, N12, and N13 in the imidazole ring, which were easily attacked by nucleophilic species (Tan et al., 2022). Besides, the ESP mapping in Fig. S12c revealed that N4 in the pyridine ring, O16 and O17 in the nitro group of IMD might be attacked by the positively charged h+ and 1O2. Otherwise, C1 and C7 in the pyridine ring, N9, C10, C11 and N12 in the imidazole might be attacked by the negatively charged O2 ·-.

Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T) was applied to evaluate the toxicity of the photodegraded intermediates. The acute toxicity (fathead minnow LC50, daphnia magna LC50 and oral rat LD50), bioconcentration factor, developmental toxicity and mutagenicity were determined by the quantitative structure-activity relationship (Zhou et al., 2022). As illustrated in Table S10, the LC50 and LD50 values of the intermediates were dramatically increased compared with IMD, indicating that the photocatalytic process significantly decreased the acute toxicity of IMD. The bioconcentration factor of some of the intermediates decreased and the developmental toxicity altered from toxicity to non-toxicity with the photodegradation of IMD. Besides, compared with mutagenicity positive pollutant IMD, the mutagenicity of the most intermediates became negative. Judging from above results, it can be identified that after the photocatalytic degradation by the CBB heterojunction, the ecological risks of IMD were significantly alleviated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of IMD in natural water
ce:section.ce:para: Given that various anions in natural water might exert influences on the generation of radicals, the impacts of common acid group anions on the photodegradation of IMD was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 8a, low concentration of chloride (Cl−) inhibited the photocatalytic degradation of IMD, which was possibly because that Cl− generated Cl· and ClOH - with low oxidizing properties and shielded the functions of SO4 ·- and ·OH in the CBB/PMS/Vis system (Cheng et al., 2020). Nevertheless, as the concentration of Cl− increased to 10 mM, the degradation rate of IMD slightly increased. This was because the accumulated Cl· further reacted with Cl− to generate Cl2 ·- and Cl2. Meanwhile, HClO and ClO were generated from ClOH - under sufficient Cl− dosage (Tan et al., 2022). These species possess strong oxidation capacity and benefit the photocatalytic degradation of IMD. The effects of bicarbonate (HCO3 −) and dihydrogen phosphate (HPO4 2−) on IMD degradation were presented in Fig. 8b and c, indicating that these two anions restrained the photocatalytic degradation of IMD. The higher the concentration of the anions was, the more significant photodegradation-inhibition appeared. The reliable reason was that the presence of HCO3 − and HPO4 2− could increase the alkalinity of the solution, under which condition SO4 ·- could be transformed to ·OH. Then HCO3 − and HPO4 2− interacted with ·OH and SO4 ·- to generate HCO3 ·-, CO3 ·- and H2PO4 ·- radicals that with weaker oxidation capability (Fang et al., 2023). Hence, the photodegradation of IMD was depressed. Comparatively, the presence of SO4 2− and NO3 − did not exert these effects, thus had negligible impact on the removal efficiency of IMD at the same dosage (Fig. 8d and e).

The influences of water chemistry conditions (i.e., pH and HA) on the photocatalytic degradation of IMD were also explored. As a result, the degradation efficiency of IMD changed insignificantly at the tested pH, except for under extremely alkaline condition (pH 10 and 11) (Fig. 8f). In this case PMS mainly existed in SO5 2− specie that hard to form SO4 ·- and ·OH, which reduced the content of free radicals and decreased the degradation efficiency of IMD. Besides, the surface of CBB was negatively charged at the tested pH. Higher pH increased the electrostatic repulsion between PMS and CBB, which was unfavorable for the photodegradation of IMD. Given that CBB exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance over a wide pH range, the presence of HA had a negative effect on the photocatalytic degradation of IMD, which was dosage-dependent (Fig. 8g). The functional groups (e.g., phenolic hydroxyls, carboxyls, and quinone) on HA surface might interact with the active sites on the CBB photocatalyst (Lee et al., 2020), therefore inhibited the activation of PMS and the photocatalytic degradation of IMD.

Based on the above analysis, the photocatalytic degradation of IMD in natural water samples was further investigated. Judging from the degradation rate and k values, the photocatalytic degradation of IMD by CBB/PMS/Vis system in different natural water samples followed the order of DI water > tap water > river water > effluent water (Fig. 8h and i). This was presumably attributable to the radical-scavenging effect caused by the anions and the active sites-shielding effect caused by the natural organic matters (NOM) containing in natural water (compositions of the water samples were listed in Table S11). The results indicate that the synthesized CBB photocatalyst can be applied to remove IMD in natural water, especially in relatively clean surface water.

In this study, a novel S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst was successfully synthesized via a facile route, which was highly effective in the photodegradation of IMD under visible light irradation via the activation of PMS in the aquatic environment. The excellent photocatalytic performance of the CBB heterojunction was attributable to the enhanced separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers, the activation of PMS by reactive species, and the intensify of light adsorption caused by CQDs. The possible IMD photodegradation pathways were confirmed through HPLC-MS method and DFT calculations, and the attenuated toxicity of the products was verified through software estimation. This work provides new insights into designing and producing ideal photocatalysts to remove NEOs that released to the aquatic environment, and the results might be further extended to emerging contaminants control and remediation field.

Sai Bai: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ting Lv: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Miaomiao Chen: Investigation. Chang Li: Writing – review & editing. Zichen Wang: Writing – review & editing. Xinyao Yang: Supervision, Resources. Tianjiao Xia: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
